General Atomics YFQ-42A drone crashes during California test flight

A General Atomics YFQ-42A Collaborative Combat Aircraft prototype crashed shortly after takeoff in the California desert on April…...

The crash of an advanced collaborative combat aircraft prototype signals potential design or integration challenges that could delay military autonomous platform development timelines.

Drones Apr 7, 2026

DJI confirms end-of-support timeline for Mavic 2 series, Matrice 600

Tech giant DJI will officially end service and support for several of its most beloved professional and prosumer drones in the coming weeks. That includes the iconic DJI Mavic 2 Pro, the versatile DJI Mavic 2 Enterprise Zoom and DJI Mavic 2 Enterprise Dual, and the heavy-lift powerhouse DJI Matrice...

DJI's discontinuation of support for flagship models signals industry shift toward newer platforms, compelling existing operators to plan costly fleet upgrades.

See DroneDJ →

Northrop Grumman Corporation (NYSE: NOC) showcased the autonomous mission capabilities of its Lumberjack™ Group 3 Uncrewed Aircraft...

Northrop Grumman's autonomous Group 3 UAS demonstrates scalable unmanned capabilities for military operations beyond traditional surveillance roles.

Crying Baby Controversy After Allegiant Deplanes Family On MedLink Advice

A couple and their newborn are removed from the plane.

Airlines' reliance on third-party medical advisors for passenger removal decisions creates liability and PR risks when interventions lack transparent medical justification.

Safety Apr 7, 2026

The Evolution of Aviation SMS

From the Wright brothers to the 2024 FAA rule — how aviation safety evolved from reactive investigation to proactive risk management.

Modern SMS shifts aviation's paradigm from investigating accidents after they occur to systematically identifying and mitigating hazards before incidents develop.

DroneShield's software advancement addresses the accelerating proliferation of hostile drone capabilities, giving defense operators critical decision-support tools to counter threats at scale.

Commercial Apr 7, 2026

Iran War Fallout: Airlines Slash Thousands Of Flights Amid Global Fuel Shortages

As the Iran crisis escalates, airlines worldwide are grappling with skyrocketing fuel costs and limited access to jet fuel.

Geopolitical disruptions in Middle East oil supplies are forcing airlines to reduce capacity rather than absorb margin-crushing fuel cost increases, signaling potential revenue impacts across the industry.

Safety Apr 7, 2026

I’m A Flight Attendant: This Is How We Handle Difficult Passengers

Always trying to resolve issues before they start.

Flight attendants employ proactive de-escalation techniques to prevent conflicts, reducing operational disruptions and crew safety incidents before they occur.

Safety Apr 7, 2026

Safety Culture in Aviation SMS

How organizational culture shapes safety outcomes and what it takes to build a reporting culture that supports SMS.

Organizations with strong psychological safety see 300% more safety reports, enabling proactive hazard identification rather than reactive incident investigation.

General Aviation Apr 7, 2026

Drought Parches Florida

The state was unusually dry for much of 2025, but the intensity of the drought has ratcheted up since January 2026.

Prolonged drought conditions may impact Florida's aviation operations through reduced water availability for aircraft cooling systems and potential restrictions on airport fire-suppression capabilities.

See NASA →

Aerospace Apr 7, 2026

Artemis 2 swings around the moon

Four astronauts from the United States and Canada became the humans to travel the furthest from the Earth April 6 as they went around the moon on the Artemis 2 mission.

Artemis 2's successful lunar flyby validates NASA's deep-space human spaceflight architecture ahead of planned surface landings, demonstrating critical systems performance for sustained lunar operations.

See SpaceNews →

Commercial Apr 7, 2026

The Problem With Ultra Long-Haul Flights Airlines Don’t Talk About

Why Air New Zealand is unable to compete on its flagship route.

Ultra long-haul routes create unsustainable crew fatigue and operational costs that smaller carriers struggle to manage profitably against established competitors.

See Simple Flying →

General Aviation Apr 7, 2026

From the Flight Deck – Eastern Iowa Airport, IA (CID)

Eastern Iowa Airport (CID) is a medium-sized multi-use airport located south of Cedar Rapids, Iowa. Traffic includes basic training aircraft up through air carrier aircraft. Flight schools often use...

Eastern Iowa Airport's diverse traffic mix from trainers to air carriers positions it as a critical hub for both pilot development and regional commercial connectivity in the Midwest.

See Federal Aviation Administration (YouTube) →

5 Fighter Jets With The Most Advanced Radar Systems

A closer look at some of the fighters with the longest striking range.

Advanced radar systems enable fighter jets to detect and engage targets at unprecedented distances, fundamentally extending combat engagement envelopes beyond visual range.

Commercial Apr 6, 2026

Air Canada Expands To The Canary Islands & Latin America, Thanks To The Airbus A321XLR & A220

The carrier is making big moves in high-yield markets.

Air Canada's ultra-long-range A321XLR and A220 deployments enable profitable transatlantic and Latin American routes previously requiring larger, less efficient widebody aircraft.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Apr 6, 2026

Here's How Air Canada's Airbus A321XLR 1-1 Lie-Flat Suites Compare To Widebody Business Class

The A321XLR's 4,700-nautical-mile range opens secondary European cities but does the 12.2-foot-wide cabin match the widebody experience?

Air Canada's A321XLR enables profitable single-aisle service to secondary European markets, but its narrowbody cabin width may limit premium product differentiation versus traditional widebody business class.

See Simple Flying →

Safety Apr 7, 2026

Surface Safety Spotlight – Los Angeles International Airport, CA (LAX) – From the Flight Deck

Los Angeles International Airport (LAX) is one of the nation’s busiest commercial airports. This short video highlights a runway incursion hazard where pilots exiting Runway 24R, particularly via...

Runway incursion risks at major hubs like LAX remain elevated during exit procedures, requiring pilots to maintain heightened situational awareness despite routine operations.

See Federal Aviation Administration (YouTube) →

Commercial Apr 6, 2026

“There’s A Huge Demand”: The Boeing 787-8 Dreamliner Demolished After Only 13 Flying Hours

A barely flown Boeing 787-8 is scrapped because its parts are worth more than the whole.

Supply chain constraints and parts shortages have created perverse economics where airlines profit more from dismantling underutilized aircraft than operating them.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Apr 6, 2026

The Cost Of Flying Premium Economy In 2026 Compared To Economy

The hidden truth about premium economy.

Premium economy pricing strategies are shifting in 2026, signaling airlines' evolving capacity management and ancillary revenue models as they recalibrate post-pandemic demand patterns.

Commercial Apr 6, 2026

The World's Largest Airline By Number Of Hubs

American Airlines reigns supreme on this front.

American Airlines' hub dominance creates structural competitive advantages in network connectivity and passenger feed that are difficult for rivals to replicate.

See Simple Flying →

Industry Apr 7, 2026

Towards Environmentally Sustainable Aviation: A Review on Operational Optimization

Growing air traffic is straining airports and airspace while raising environmental concerns, and demand is only expected to increase. This paper reviews how advanced technologies and methods can expand capacity and improve efficiency across airline, airport, and flight operations. It also highlights emerging trends and gaps for future research in sustainable aviation.

Operational optimization across integrated airline-airport-airspace systems offers immediate efficiency gains that can accommodate projected traffic growth without proportional environmental impact increases.

See MDPI →

Drones Apr 6, 2026

China tests heavy cargo drone designed for extreme terrain

China has completed the maiden flight of a new 7-tonne autonomous cargo aircraft, taking a...

China's 7-tonne autonomous cargo drone expands last-mile logistics capabilities in mountainous regions where traditional aircraft cannot operate economically.

Aerospace Apr 6, 2026

NASA’s Artemis II Crew Eclipses Record for Farthest Human Spaceflight

Editor’s note: This release was updated at 7:28 p.m. EDT on April 6, 2026, to reflect Orion’s final closest approach to the lunar surface from about 4,070 miles to about 4,067 miles. NASA also updated the farthest distance Orion will travel. Finally, NASA also made changes at 10 p.m. to the...

Artemis II's lunar trajectory achievements demonstrate sustained progress in deep-space human spaceflight capabilities essential for future long-duration missions beyond Earth orbit.

See NASA →

Aerospace Apr 6, 2026

NASA’s Northrop Grumman CRS-24 Mission Overview

NASA’s Northrop Grumman Commercial Resupply Services 24 mission, or Northrop Grumman CRS-24, will deliver approximately 11,000 pounds of science and supplies to the International Space Station. This mission will be the second flight of the Cygnus XL, the larger, more cargo-capable version of the...

Northrop Grumman's larger Cygnus XL variant demonstrates increased ISS resupply capacity, enabling more frequent or consolidated cargo missions that could reduce overall launch frequency and operational costs.

See NASA →

General Aviation Apr 6, 2026

K-State Salina Launches High School Aviation Camp

Weeklong program will include simulator work, ground school and up to three hours of flight time.

Hands-on flight exposure during high school years can significantly accelerate pilot certification timelines and build critical decision-making skills before college aviation programs.

See AVweb →

Aerospace Apr 6, 2026

Moog Highlights Growing Satellite Bus Capabilities with Full‑Scale METEOR Reveal at Space Symposium

East Aurora, NY — Moog Inc. (NYSE: MOG.A and MOG.B), a worldwide designer, manufacturer and systems integrator of high-performance precision motion and fluid controls and control systems will highlight its […]...

Moog's METEOR satellite bus platform signals aerospace suppliers' shift toward standardized modular architectures to reduce constellation deployment costs and accelerate small-satellite production.

See SpaceNews →

Aerospace Apr 6, 2026

FOSSA targets Japan’s defense market as larger smallsats expand capabilities

Spanish startup FOSSA Systems is pushing into Japan’s defense market after securing a local partner to expand its reach, building on a shift beyond tiny picosatellites used to connect low-power monitoring devices toward more capable spacecraft for broader government applications.

FOSSA's evolution from basic IoT connectivity to defense-grade satellites signals how smallsat operators are capturing government contracts traditionally held by larger aerospace primes.

See SpaceNews →

General Aviation Apr 6, 2026

Air Tractor Holdings Acquires Thrush Aircraft

Deal combines two ag aviation manufacturers under shared ownership.

The consolidation creates an integrated agricultural aviation provider with complementary aircraft portfolios and manufacturing capabilities, potentially reshaping competitive dynamics in the niche ag-spray market.

See AVweb →

General Aviation Apr 6, 2026

Hartzell Expands Top Prop Program

Update adds 150+ models, pricing changes for GA aircraft upgrades.

Hartzell's expanded propeller compatibility across 150+ GA models signals increased aftermarket modernization demand as owners prioritize performance upgrades over new aircraft purchases.

See AVweb →

General Aviation Apr 6, 2026

Poll: Will Phoenix Trigger Broader Landing Fees?

Could Phoenix-area landing fees spark broader GA change?

Phoenix's landing fee implementation may establish a precedent that pressures other municipalities to monetize airport infrastructure, potentially increasing operational costs across the general aviation network.

See AVweb →

General Aviation Apr 6, 2026

Landing Fees and Flight Training in Phoenix

As Falcon Field and Mesa Gateway add landing fees for light aircraft, officials and flight schools describe different futures for the Phoenix area.

Rising landing fees at Phoenix's training airports may consolidate flight training operations and reduce accessibility for cost-sensitive general aviation students.

Aerospace Apr 6, 2026

Night Sky Network Celebrates Artemis II

Today, the crew of Artemis II reaches a milestone, traveling farther than any humans in recorded history, as they orbit the far side of the Moon, roughly 4,000 miles above the lunar surface. You can watch this historic broadcast on NASA’s YouTube channel today, April 6, 2026, at 10 AM Pacific (1 PM...

Artemis II's lunar orbit milestone demonstrates NASA's sustained deep-space capability, signaling renewed human spaceflight momentum beyond low-Earth orbit operations.

See NASA →

Grants

Grants Status Requests To submit a request, visit NASA General Information Request Form and complete the form. You will receive an automated email with the most commonly requested grant status information. Important Instructions: How to Fill Out the Form: Memorandum for NASA Grantee Community...

NASA's streamlined grant status request system through automated forms enables faster administrative processing for aerospace researchers and organizations managing active research projects.

Military Apr 6, 2026

Lady of Larissa Flies Again: Incredible New Footage Emerges of the Secretive RQ-180 Spy Drone

New videos give an even clearer view – unquestionably our best ever – of the legendary classified stealth drone, previously seen landing in Greece last month. Last month, we reported on images taken in Larissa, Greece, showing what we, and others, concluded – based on prior sightings and comment...

Recent high-resolution footage of the RQ-180 stealth drone provides the clearest evidence yet of this classified platform's operational deployment, potentially signaling increased intelligence collection activities in Eastern Europe.

Aerospace Apr 6, 2026

The Near Side of the Moon

The astronauts aboard NASA’s Orion spacecraft captured this photo of the Moon’s near side on April 4, 2026. The image features dark patches at center and right; these are ancient lava flows, which are unique to this side of the Moon. The Artemis II astronauts – astronauts Victor Glover, Reid...

Artemis II's lunar imagery demonstrates NASA's progress toward sustained Moon operations, validating spacecraft systems for crewed deep-space missions beyond Earth orbit.
